does: ARES presents a two-stage algorithm that alternates between reinforcement learning and supervised fine-tuning to improve multimodal chain-of-thought reasoning. In the first stage, the method uses a teacher AI model to provide sentence-level feedback scoring how much each sentence contributes to solving problems, enabling granular reward signals for RL training. In the second stage, after RL refinement, the teacher model identifies and corrects remaining reasoning errors, which are used to further fine-tune the model via supervised learning. By leveraging detailed AI feedback at the sentence level rather than only ranking entire generations, ARES achieves more targeted model improvement while avoiding common RL artifacts like repetitive outputs.
connects: This work builds on established approaches in aligning large language models through human and AI feedback, particularly RLHF and RLAIF methods that have proven effective for preference alignment. It extends prior work on chain-of-thought reasoning—a key technique for improving reasoning in both language and multimodal models—by combining it with fine-grained feedback mechanisms. The paper connects to recent advances in using capable teacher models like GPT-4 and Claude to generate various forms of detailed feedback, and responds to limitations in existing ranking-based feedback approaches by introducing sentence-level annotations that provide more informative learning signals during training.
enables: ARES opens pathways for more efficient alignment of multimodal models by demonstrating how granular, AI-generated feedback can accelerate improvement beyond ranking-only methods. The approach suggests a broader framework for iteratively combining RL and SFT stages with different feedback types, which could be applied to other reasoning tasks and multimodal settings. By reducing the need for human-provided detailed feedback while improving sample efficiency through sentence-level rewards, this work enables scaling alignment techniques to larger models and more complex reasoning problems. Future research could extend this alternating framework to other domains, explore optimal teacher model selection, or investigate how to automatically determine which feedback types are most valuable for different problem categories.
